data.title: Trump to Unveil 'Major Trade Deal' with UK After Tariff Blitz
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to announce a trade deal with the UK on Thursday, according to media reports. This would make the UK the first country to secure an agreement following the White House’s recent tariffs. The news follows a social media post by Trump, in which he teased the announcement of a “major trade deal” with a “big and highly respected country.”

Earlier, a UK official confirmed on Tuesday that both countries have made substantial progress toward a trade deal, which is expected to include reduced tariff quotas on steel and automobiles. Notably, UK car and steel exports to the U.S. are currently subject to a 25% tariff.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration had previously confirmed that it is actively negotiating trade deals with India, the UK, South Korea, and Japan.

Below are some of the UK stocks affected by Trump’s tariffs.

data.author: Kirti Tak

data.title: Stock Market News Today – Indices Close Higher As Fed Holds Rates Steady, Trump Scraps Biden-era Chip Restrictions
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) and the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) closed in the green following a news-driven day of trading.

For starters, the Fed held the federal funds rate steady between 4.25% and 4.50% in an unanimous decision. After the decision was publicized, Fed Chair Jerome Powell held a news conference, saying that Trump’s reciprocal tariffs, if enacted at the rates set on “Liberation Day,” could lead to higher inflation and lower unemployment. Powell also stressed that the Fed is taking on a wait and see approach in regard to monetary policy and the tariff situation.

Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is preparing to meet with top Chinese trade officials in Switzerland over the weekend. Ahead of the meeting, Trump noted today that he would not preemptively lower the U.S.’s 145% tariffs on Chinese goods in order to get China on board with more material negotiations.

In terms of more positive tariff developments, Vietnam's trade minister, Nguyen Hong Dien, encouraged Vietnamese companies to increase trade with the U.S. Last year, the U.S. had a $123.5 billion trade deficit with Vietnam.

It was also a busy day for two Magnificent 7 companies: Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA).

GOOG stock finished the day down by 7.51% after Apple’s (AAPL) senior vice president of services, Eddy Cue, revealed that Apple was exploring the integration of AI search engines into its Safari browser.

As for Nvidia, a Bloomberg report revealed that the Trump administration would scrap Biden-era restrictions on AI chip exports and replace them with new rules. NVDA stock closed higher by 3.10% on the news.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Trump to Withdraw Biden's Chip Export Restrictions, Create New Rules
data.description:
Chips stocks received a boost following the Trump administration’s plans to rescind AI chip restrictions set by the Biden administration, according to a new report from Bloomberg.

The export controls, called the AI Diffusion Rule, was set to be enacted on May 15, creating a three-tier system for chip exports.

AI Diffusion Rule Will Not Be Enforced on May 15

While the AI Diffusion Rule will no longer enforced on the 15th, Trump has plans to create a new set of chip restriction rules.

“The Biden AI rule is overly complex, overly bureaucratic, and would stymie American innovation. We will be replacing it with a much simpler rule that unleashes American innovation and ensures American AI dominance," said a spokesperson from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Industry and Security.

AI chip leader Nvidia (NVDA) closed higher by 3% on the news.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Powell: High Tariffs Could Result in Higher Inflation, Lower Employment
data.description:
Following the Fed’s decision to leave rates between 4.25% and 4.50%, Fed Chair Jerome Powell stated that if President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs take effect, inflation could rise while employment could fall. He also said that there is a significant amount of uncertainty surrounding the tariffs. As a result, the Fed is taking on a wait and see and data-driven approach in regard to monetary policy.

Powell Willing to Be Patient in Policy Decisions

At the same time, Powell added that the Fed has to power to move quickly “when things develop,” but did not provide a timeframe. For now, Powell is comfortable with the Fed’s policy stance as the global economy waits for the tariff situation to play out.

As usual, the S&P 500 (SPX) is exhibiting volatile price action following the Fed rate decision.

Disclaimer & DisclosureReport an Issue
data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Trump Not Willing to Preemptively Lower 145% Tariffs on China Ahead of Trade Talks
data.description:
During the swearing-in ceremony for the U.S.’s new ambassador to China, President Trump was asked if he would preemptively lower the 145% tariff on Chinese goods in order to get China on board with more material negotiations. His simple response? “No.”

Trump Plays Hard Ball Ahead of U.S.-China Trade Talks

It doesn’t appear that Trump is completely against lowering the tariffs on China, as he noted last week that “145% is very high and it won't be that high” in a question-and-answer session in the Oval Office. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is set to meet with key Chinese trade officials this weekend in an encouraging development.

The tariff drama has impacted markets across the globe, with the S&P 500 (SPX) down by 4.67% this year.

Disclaimer & DisclosureReport an Issue
data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Fed Leaves Rates Unchanged at 4.25% to 4.50%, Says Uncertainty has 'Increased Further'
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has left the federal funds rate unchanged at 4.25% to 4.50%, as expected. Prior to the meeting, the CME FedWatch tool had assigned a 97.7% probability that the rate would remain constant. That probability was much lower at 61.5% a month ago, surging higher as positive economic data flowed in. The target rate has now remained unchanged since December.

Fed Keeps Rates at 4.25% to 4.50%

In a press release, the Fed noted that the unemployment rate has stabilized to a low level in recent months while “Uncertainty about the economic outlook has increased further.”

Investors can keep up with other key economic data with TipRank’s Economic Indicators Dashboard.

*This is a developing story. Stay tuned for additional updates.*

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: Vietnam Seeks to Boost U.S. Imports Amid Trade Talks
data.description:
Vietnam’s trade minister, Nguyen Hong Dien, has issued a statement encouraging Vietnam companies to increase their trade with the U.S., according to a report from Bloomberg. The country seeks to purchase more high-value U.S. products at a steady and sustainable pace, in industries like energy, mining, and aviation.

Vietnam Encourages Trade with the U.S.

Last month, Trump placed a 46% tariff on the southeast Asia country before suspending it for 90 days. In 2024, the U.S. exported $13.1 billion of goods to Vietnam and imported $136.6 billion of Vietnamese goods, equivalent to a $123.5 billion trade deficit for the U.S.

Interestingly, the VanEck Vietnam ETF (VNM) has returned nearly 7% this year, outperforming the S&P 500’s loss of 4.2%.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: S&P 500 Index Today: SPY Stock Trades in Range Ahead of Fed Rate Decision
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) opened higher by 0.25% this morning and has been subject to range-bound price action ahead of the Fed interest rate decision. The Fed is widely expected to leave the federal funds rate unchanged between 4.25% and 4.50%, with the CME FedWatch tool assigning a 97.7% probability to this outcome.

In tariff news, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced today that he will meet with trade representatives from China in Switzerland on Saturday. Perhaps the market has already priced in this news and is now waiting for an actual trade deal.

Additionally, the EU’s top trade representative said that the EU will announce preparatory measures to counter U.S. tariffs tomorrow, May 8.

The S&P 500 is up by 0.44% at the time of writing, softening its year-to-date loss to 4.67%.

Which Stocks are Moving the S&P 500?

Let's pivot to the TipRanks S&P 500 heatmap, which illustrates the stocks that have contributed to the index's price action. A quick scan shows that most stocks are in the green.

Front and center is Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), which is down by 8% on news that Apple (AAPL) plans on introducing AI search options into its Safari browser. Meanwhile, Amazon (AMZN) is up by 2% after Tigress Financial raised its AMZN price target to $305 from $290.

SPY Stock Moves Higher with the S&P 500

The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is an exchange-traded fund designed to track the movement of the S&P 500. As a result, SPY moves in tandem with SPX.

Wall Street expects further upside for SPY. During the past three months, analysts have issued an average SPY price target of $658.54 for the stocks within the index, implying upside of 17.25% from current prices. The 504 stocks in the SPY carry 409 buy ratings, 88 hold ratings, and 7 sell ratings.

data.title: Household Electronics at Risk of 30% Price Jump from Tariffs
data.description:
Household electronics, like laptops, tablets, monitors, and smartphones, could be subject to a 30% or higher rise in prices, according to a new report from the WSJ that cites new data from the Consumer Technology Association (CTA). The data assumes a 25% tariff on these products.

Laptops and Smartphones Could Get Much More Expensive

The CTA data also shows that video game console prices could increase by a staggering 69.4%, while computer accessories, desktop computers, and speakers and headphones prices could rise by over 20%.

Many consumer electronics stocks have suffered steep declines this year.

data.author: Eddie Pan

data.title: J.P. Morgan Warns Tariff Chaos Could Slash Global GDP 1% as U.S. Stocks Struggle
data.description:
J.P. Morgan's (JPM) latest analysis on U.S. tariffs raises more questions than answers, especially when it comes to the global economic outlook. While President Trump's trade moves have been causing plenty of disruptions, J.P. Morgan analysts believe the impact is far from straightforward. The firm's commentary signals that tariffs might push global GDP down by 1%, but that could be just the tip of the iceberg.

Tariff Woes Could Lower Global GDP by 1%

J.P. Morgan has warned that a global GDP reduction of up to 1% is possible if the trade war escalates, particularly with the U.S. slapping 10% tariffs on goods from all trading partners and ramping up those on China to 104%. Joseph Lupton, a global economist at J.P. Morgan, highlighted how the knock-on effects, including weakened business sentiment, could make this damage even worse. These tariffs could lead to higher costs for U.S. consumers, which, in turn, dampens spending and risks dragging both U.S. and global growth.

S&P 500 Likely to Stay in a Tight Range

Despite the trade war turbulence, J.P. Morgan's analysts aren't expecting an immediate crash in the stock market. Instead, they're forecasting a “range-bound” S&P 500. Fabio Bassi, head of Cross-Asset Strategy at J.P. Morgan, said, "We expect the S&P 500 to be constrained to the lower end of our range." This sentiment echoes the concern that mixed tariff news, combined with President Trump's unpredictable moves, will keep markets in a holding pattern. Investors are bracing for a scenario where trade deals don't materialize quickly enough to boost sentiment.

As indicated by the TipRanks graphic below, the S&P 500 has decreased by nearly 7% over the past three months. This drop reflects a turbulent market period (SPX). Investors are digesting the ongoing uncertainty surrounding tariffs and their global impact.

Will a Recession Follow? J.P. Morgan Thinks It's Possible

Adding to the uncertainty, J.P. Morgan's analysts see a 40% chance of a global recession by the end of 2025. Bruce Kasman, J.P. Morgan's Chief Global Economist, noted, "The uncertainty surrounding tariffs is weighing on business confidence and could accelerate the push toward a downturn." This isn't just about numbers on paper. The actual business sentiment has been dipping, and that means companies may hold back on investments and hiring. If that happens, it could further stifle growth and deepen the recession risk.

Mixed Signals Make Tariffs More Complex Than Expected

In a world where the trade policy landscape shifts almost daily, J.P. Morgan's analysis shows how tariffs are more than just taxes—they're changing the way the global economy works. In fact, GDP forecasts are being slashed and business confidence is at a low, this ongoing tariff drama is likely to have ripple effects for years. The situation remains pretty fluid, so its important to note that J.P. Morgan's cautious outlook paints a picture of a turbulent but range-bound future. The real question is, will things ever calm down, or are we stuck in this cycle?

data.author: Annika Masrani
